paragraphs.text: Then Chedorlaomer (Khodologomor), king of Elam, with three allies came to make war with the king of Sodom and his four allies, put the king of Sodom and his allies to flight, seized all the wealth of Sodom and Lot with his possessions, and withdrew. Then Abram took three hundred and eighteen of his servants and, together with Aner and his allies, pursued after the king of Elam, smote him, and brought back the captives with their possessions, and also Lot, his nephew, with all his estate. And since the goods of the Sodomites had been mingled with the goods of the conquered kings, Abram refused the spoil taken from the kings he had defeated.

“And Melchizedek king of Salem brought forth bread and wine: and he was the priest of the most high God. And he blessed Abram, and said: …Blessed be the most high God, who hath delivered thine enemies into thy hand. And Abram gave him tithes of all” (Gen 14:18–20). This Melchizedek was Shem; in his greatness he was a king, as the forefather of fourteen tribes, but also a high priest, because by succession he received the priesthood from his father Noah. Shem lived not only to the time of Abram, as Scripture says, but even to Jacob and Esau, the sons of Abram’s son. Rebekah went to inquire of him, and Shem said to her that two nations were in her womb, and the elder would serve the younger (Gen 25:22–23). But Rebekah would not have gone to inquire of him, passing by her husband, who had been so miraculously delivered from the slaughter, and her father-in-law, to whom such frequent divine revelations had come, had she not known of his greatness from Abram himself and from his son.

And Abram would not have given Melchizedek tithes, had he not known that he was incomparably higher than himself. It cannot be supposed that Rebekah would have gone to inquire of any of the Canaanites or Sodomites, and that to any of them Abram would have given a tithe of the estate he had recovered. Since the days of Melchizedek’s life extended into the times of Jacob and Esau, what is said is probable, namely that Shem also appeared to them. His father Noah lived in the east, but Shem dwelt among two generations, that is, among the sons of Ham and his own sons, forming as it were a barrier between one tribe and the other, for he feared that the descendants of Ham might draw his sons into idolatry.
